comment: Refused to answer and did not list any of the required RAG components; citations are related to LangChain but do not support the gold answer.
Listed generic LangChain app features like streaming and memory instead of the canonical RAG components requested by the gold rule.
Correctly named multiple RAGAS metrics and explained their meanings; evidence directly supports the answer.
Correctly answered the metric names and brief explanations with direct support from the cited evidence.
Covered core LangGraph capabilities and clearly stated its lower-level orchestration relationship with LangChain.
Answered both the core functions and the LangGraph-LangChain relationship clearly enough to satisfy the rule.
Correctly described FAISS as a vector similarity search library but failed to clearly connect it to the RAG retrieval/indexing role.
Got FAISS general purpose right but still stopped short of explaining its concrete role inside a RAG system.
Correctly identified Self-RAG and explained the self-reflection mechanism as the key innovation.
Answered CRAG instead of Self-RAG, so both the answer and citations miss the target method.
Refused the question instead of extracting major technical directions from the available survey-style material.
Recovered several high-level directions such as taxonomy, tradeoffs, applications, and practices, but not the intended core technical directions like reflection and tool use.
Only recommended RAGFlow and did not meet the minimum project count required by the rubric.
Mentioned two concrete open-source items, A-RAG and RAGFlow, but still fell short of a fuller project recommendation list.
Avoided an absolute conclusion and discussed several methods, but did not clearly compare their respective strengths and tradeoffs by scenario.
Compared multiple methods cautiously but still did not explicitly map them to their characteristic strengths like self-reflection, correction, or hierarchy.
Did not produce the required design comparison and instead stopped at saying there was not enough evidence.
Still failed to form the required LangChain versus LlamaIndex comparison and defaulted to an insufficiency answer.
Correctly described Self-RAG as internal self-reflection and CRAG as post-retrieval correction, and highlighted the key distinction.
Refused the comparison even though the available evidence was enough for a concise mechanism-level distinction.
Identified Dify and RAGFlow at a basic level but did not clearly state their positioning difference as platform versus enterprise RAG engine.
Recovered some product characteristics for both sides but still stopped short of a clean positioning contrast.
Noted that the SoK paper is taxonomy-oriented, but the comparison against the survey paper remained too vague.
Recognized the evidence gap yet still provided only a weak comparison instead of clearly stating the two classification perspectives.
Did not perform the allowed manual grouping of the paper list, so the type-distribution task remains unfinished.
Also refused to manually classify the paper list, which is the core requirement of this question.
Correctly summarized three major RAG improvement directions, but the rubric expects roughly four to five.
Only gave two directions and one of them drifted into a vague best-practices category instead of the intended method families.
Correctly classified CrewAI and LangGraph as agent frameworks, but failed to complete the full four-project classification.
Also only completed the agent-framework half of the classification and left the platform half unresolved.
Calculation process and final difference value are both correct.
Calculation and conclusion are correct and directly supported by the cited table.
Recovered two valid acquisition methods, arXiv API and Fetcher, but still missed the direct urllib request path required for full credit.
Only identified Fetcher and missed the other main acquisition methods in the crawler script.
Missed the relevant crawler code chunk entirely and therefore failed both the function-parameter and metadata parts.
Also failed to retrieve the relevant code evidence, so the answer and citations do not support the gold result.
Computed the increases correctly and reasonably reported that context recall is among the largest gains, with the table supporting the result.
Computed the numeric gains correctly but concluded context precision alone was largest, which is weaker than the gold interpretation centered on context recall.
Correctly argued that the evidence is insufficient for a universal superiority claim and gave multiple concrete reasons.
Also gave a cautious evidence-bounded conclusion with multiple concrete supporting reasons.
